title: Magnetic field effects on spin-split band and magnon transport in altermagnets and emergent compensated ferrimagnets
abstract: In altermagnets and fully compensated ferrimagnets, not only the electron band but also the magnon band exhibits spin splitting without net magnetization, which enables thermal activation of the magnon spin current. Here, we theoretically investigate magnetic field effects on the magnon properties of these antiferromagnets in the presence of a weak easy-axis anisotropy which makes the collinear states robust against the magnetic field. For the altermagnet and compensated ferrimagnet, we analyze a 2 sublattice order in the $J_1$-$J_2$-$J_2^\prime$ model on the square lattice and a triple-${\bf Q}$ 12-sublattice order in the $J_1$-$J_3$ model on the kagome lattice, respectively, each accompanied by $d$-wave and $s$-wave spin splitting at zero field. It is shown that for positive (negative) magnetic field $H$ whose energy scale is smaller than the anisotropy gap, the up- and down-spin magnon bands are shifted to lower (higher) and higher (lower) energies, respectively, similarly to the Zeeman coupling in electron systems. In the altermagnet, with increasing field, the $d$-wave splitting tends to be deformed into the $s$-wave one, which is reflected as the change in the direction of the spin current generated by thermal gradient. In the compensated ferrimagnet, the $s$-wave nature, i.e., the population imbalance between the up- and down-spin magnons at $H=0$, results in an asymmetric field dependence of the longitudinal spin and thermal conductivities.
summary: This theoretical work examines how magnetic fields influence spin-split magnon bands in altermagnets and compensated ferrimagnets. By analyzing the magnon Hamiltonian and calculating transport coefficients, the authors demonstrate that the field induces Zeeman-like shifts and that the specific magnetic symmetry dictates the resulting spin and thermal current response. This is crucial for understanding spin-charge/heat transport in complex magnetic materials.
main_problem: The paper investigates how an external magnetic field affects the spin-split magnon band structure and the resulting spin and thermal transport properties in altermagnets and compensated ferrimagnets.
main_result: The study shows that the magnetic field induces Zeeman-like shifts in the magnon bands, and the specific magnetic structure dictates whether the resulting spin/thermal conductivity exhibits an asymmetric field dependence or a purely longitudinal response.
method: The analysis uses theoretical modeling, transforming the spin Hamiltonian into a Bogoliubov magnon Hamiltonian, and calculating transport coefficients ($\chi^{SC}, \kappa$) using linear response theory.
model_or_system: The systems modeled are altermagnets (using the $J_1-J_2-J'_2$ model on a square lattice) and compensated ferrimagnets (using the $J_1-J_3$ model on a kagome lattice). Both exhibit spin splitting without net magnetization.
key_observables:
Spin-conductivity ($\chi^{SC}_{\mu
u}$), thermal conductivity ($\kappa_{\mu
u}$), magnon band dispersion, and spin polarization of magnon bands.
important_parameters: External magnetic field ($H$), easy-axis anisotropy ($D$), and lattice coupling constants ($J_1, J_2, J_3$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the energy scale of the magnetic field is smaller than the anisotropy gap, and transport coefficients are calculated using the relaxation-time approximation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the field dependence of magnon bands (showing shifts), and the resulting spin/thermal currents driven by thermal gradients in both altermagnetic and ferrimagnetic states.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the physical models and Hamiltonians for the two magnetic systems, to calculating the magnon band structure and spin polarization, and finally analyzing the resulting spin and thermal transport coefficients as a function of magnetic field and temperature.
why_interesting: The study connects fundamental magnetic ordering (altermagnetism/ferrimagnetism) to measurable transport phenomena (spin/heat currents) under external fields, providing theoretical insights into emergent quantum magnet behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic pictures of the models and their collinear antiferromagnetic ground states at zero ﬁeld, where the mag- netic unit cells are indicated by dotted lines. (a) The 2- sublattice altermagnetic state in the J1-J2-J′ 2 model on the square lattice, where cyan, green, and orange lines represent the exchange interactions J1, J2, and J′ 2, respectively. (b) The 12-sublattice state with compensated ferrimagnetic nature in the J1-J3 model on the kagome lattice, where cyan and green curves represent J1 and J3, respectively. In (b), pink and cyan upward triangles possess the local magnetization of +1 (two-up and one-down) and −3 (three-down), respectively. In each of (a) and (b), numbers...

FIG. 2: Temperature and magnetic-ﬁeld phase diagram of the easy-axis altermagnet on the square lattice with J2/J1 = −0.5, J′ 2/J1 = −0.25, and D/J1 = 0.02. Yellow and cyan regions denote the stability regions of the easy-axis collinear and spin-ﬂop states, respectively. The result is obtained in the MC simulation.

FIG. 3: Field dependence of the magnon band in the alter- magnetic collinear state with the 2 sublattice structure (see the yellow region in Fig. 2). (a)-(c) The magnon dispersions at H/J1 = 0, +0.3, and −0.3 (from top to bottom) along the path connecting high symmetry points shown in Fig. 3 (d), where the color represents the spin polarization of the magnon hn,q (reddish and bluish colors represent up and down spins, respectively). In each ﬁgure, the right panel shows the cross section of the magnon band at ǫn,q/J1 = 2, where the box enclosed by the dashed lines corresponds to the one near the Γ point in (d). In (d), the inner diamond denotes the Bril- louin zone. The interaction...

FIG. 5: A schematic image of the origin of the longitudinal and transverse responses in the altermagnetic states at (a) H/J1 = 0 and (b) H/J1 = +0.3, where magenta and cyan arrows represent the ﬂow of the positively and negatively po- larized magnons, respectively.

FIG. 4: Spin and thermal currents driven by thermal gradient in the altermagnetic state [see the system setup shown in the inset of (b)]. (a) Temperature dependence of the spin-current conductivity χSC µν and (b) that of the thermal conductivity κµν, where black, reddish, and bluish solid (dashed) curves represent to the longitudinal xx (transverse xy) components at H/J1 = 0, +0.3, and −0.3, respectively. Other parameters are the same as those in Figs. 2 and 3. In (b), the xx com- ponents for H/J1 = ±0.3 (magenta and cyan curves) exactly overlap. (c) and (d) Associated ﬁeld dependences of (c) χSC µν and (d) κµν at T/J1 = 0.4. χSC µν and κµν are normalized by S3J1/γ and S4J2 1 /γ,...
